Alright, custom nutrition. So time to have some fun. Let's jump into custom nutrition. We're going to be using our blueprint that we just went over and we are going to get into more detail as to how it works and how we are going to fill it out for our clients. So the blueprint, what does it do? It calculates their BMR, right? It multiplies it by an activity level of 1.2 which is considered sedentary. Keep in mind that if you think the person is very active or something, you can multiply it by a higher activity level, maybe 1.4, 1.6, 1.8. But we're using 1.2 because the majority of all of our clients coming in are relatively sedentary. And by doing that, it finds the calories needed to maintain their current weight. Okay? Then we subtract 500 calories to create a deficit. Okay? That's 3500 total we're subtracting for the week or one pound of fat. Okay? This right here is where you can make it more or less aggressive based on your judgment. If you want to subtract 300 calories, if you want to subtract a thousand calories, that is up to you. Okay? That's why it's completely customizable. But the template subtracts 500 calories. Now, if they have a goal of gaining weight, then you can add calories to their maintenance plus 500. Right? If they're bulking. So again, it's totally customizable based on what you want. But the template is designed to subtract 500 based on a typical weight loss. But as long as you understand how it works, you should have no problem customizing it based on your beliefs and judgments. So next, we are going to break the calories up throughout the day, using the path of least resistance for max compliance. So typically when I get to this part, which we're going to be going into a sample now, but when we when we get to this part, I typically ask them, hey, currently, what is your, your largest meal of the day and what is your smallest meal of the day? So if they say something like, well, I typically skip breakfast and I eat a small lunch and then I have a huge dinner. I'm going to say, well, we're not going to do that. But what we will do is we're going to start off slow baby steps, right? So instead of making breakfast 700 calories, your largest meal of the day, I know that's going to be really overwhelming for you to eat at first. So we're not going to do that. Let's start off something small. We'll have 300 calories for breakfast, just maybe a protein shake and an apple or something like that, right? Something really, you know, relatively easy for them to begin with. And this approach, the path of least resistance usually gets them to execute it the best, right? And if they're largest meal, if they're used to eating a thousand calories for dinner, still keeping that their largest meal of the day, maybe it's not going to be a thousand, but maybe it's 700 calories or depending on how many calories they have, but keeping it their largest meal of the day will typically help them execute the program so much better. So it's not going to be a perfect, you know, even split with a customized plan because we can cater it more to that person's already existing lifestyle. And typically people will get better results by doing that. So finally, we're going to break after we break up the calories throughout the day, we're going to break it up by macros. Okay, we're going to break it up based on your judgment. It's preset at 40, 40, 20. So 40% carbs, 40% protein, 20% fat. Those are the preset macros, but of course it's a customizable plan. You can make it 40, 30, 20 if you want them to have more fat, maybe, I'm sorry, it would be 40, 30, 30 in that case. If you want them to have more fat, however you want to break it up, you can totally break it up like that. Then we are going to, whoops, then we are going to optionally, you don't have to do this part, but you can find the grams for them if you'd like to tell them how many grams they need to consume of each one of those macros. So to do that, pretty simple, you're going to take the total amount of calories you're giving them. So we'll use an example of 1400 and you're going to multiply it by the percentage macro that you're going to be giving for those calories. As an example, I want you to eat 40% of your calories from protein. Okay, well then you take 1400 calories and you multiply it by 40% or 0.4 and that is 560 calories. You're saying, hey, I want 560 calories of your food to come from protein. So there are four calories in a gram of proteins. There's four calories in a gram of carbs and there's nine calories in a gram of fat. So in our example, you would take 560 calories and you divide it by four and they have 140 grams of protein. You can write that on the paper. Again, keep in mind this, you don't need to do this calculation. It's totally optional. Some people just like to know how many grams they get. So if they do, if they ask you about it, you can do that calculation fairly simply for them just by doing a little bit of math, but you don't need to. The final step is having them download an app like my FitnessPal to track their nutrition and then send it to you on a weekly basis. Okay, so there's a lot of apps out there that track calories and macros throughout the day and whichever one is easiest for them to use compliance wise unless you really have a preference. I really like my FitnessPal. I can look at weekly views. I know how to use it really well and it's free. So I typically tell them, hey, I want you to download my FitnessPal even if you are using something else because I can coach you better that way, but it's completely up to you as long as you get the information that you are looking for from them. So let's take a look at the blueprint now. So this is a sample blueprint that I have for you guys and I filled it out and basically it is going through the calculation for a woman. So you can see here on the top and again this is the Harris-Benedic equation to find BMR and it's very widely used throughout the industry, but we are finding the there's two different formulas for one is for women, one is for men and the first number I always had questions about this. This one is 655 and this one is 66. It's not a typo. The reason that they are so much different is because the multiplying factors within the equation itself are a lot different. So anyhow, it's fairly simple how you fill this out. You just put in their weight, so 180 pounds, then you put in their height in inches. So my example was 5'7", so 67 inches and then you will put in their age, 40 years old. So now you do the math. Okay, 4.35 times 180, you drop that number down. 4.7 times 67, you drop that number down. 4.7 times 40, you drop that number down. Now we're moving on to step number two, do the math. So 655 plus 783 plus 315 minus 188, that is their BMR. So this person's BMR is 1565. So that's essentially their basal metabolic rate when they are at rest. We are going to multiply that by an activity factor of 1.2 like we talked about. So 1878 is approximately how many calories they need to maintain their current weight, 1878. So now we need to change those calories because they have goals of probably not just maintaining their current weight. So 1878, we are going to subtract 500 calories and that is how we got to 1378. That's our number right here, that's our magic number. Okay, that's how many calories we want them to consume every day, seven days a week. So how are we going to break that up? We're going to go into phase number two, break it down by meal. Okay, so like I said, in my example, the person was not used to eating breakfast and they were typically used to eating a larger dinner. So we are going to keep that relatively the same for them. So 300 calories for breakfast, 400 calories for lunch, 500 calories for dinner. That gave us 200 calories left over. You might have to play around with the numbers a little bit to get it to work out really well, but that gives us two 100 calorie snacks. I would probably recommend them drinking protein shakes and they can keep their protein high at about 100 and calories. If they go over by 20 calories a day, it's not the end of the world, okay? But the fact that they're getting, if they're doing this and they're tracking it and they are tracking their macros, they're doing an amazing job. So here's the part where you can explain to them like, look, you can eat whatever you want for your meals. And I'll give you some suggestions as I coach you, but you can, if you can make it fit, you're going to get results, right? If you can eat 1378 calories and you can break it up throughout the day and you can stick to your macros, you're going to get amazing results. Okay, so finally, when you're on to this part, when they're tracking it in my fitness pal, and if I was explaining this in person, I might break out my fitness pal on my phone and show them how to do it. But show them how to track, how to enter something into for breakfast, how to enter something in for lunch, show them what the macros look like. So that's when you explain to them like, look, I want you to eat 40% carbs, 40% protein and 20% fat. If you want to cross it off and put different percentages in for yourself, absolutely, you can probably even modify the blueprint if you wanted to. And then you can see I wrote the grams next to it as well, just as a little helpful hint. So that final piece, tracking the macros, usually the app will do all the work. They just put in the food and they click on the button that says macros and it tells them where they're at and say like, look, if you're really high on carbs for the day, maybe for dinner, you know, you need to have some protein, right? Or so on. This is where you explain that like, you can't have alcohol during this challenge or during this time period because you're never going to get it to fit your macros. And you want to make sure that you're not eating late night snacks because you don't have room for it, right? So this is where you really talk a lot about anything specific that you like to that you believe in and you can make it super customizable that way. But this is the idea of it and typically if they can do these three things, they're going to get phenomenal results tracking their calories, the breakdown throughout the day and then macros. So that is all we're going to go over for customizable nutrition. And now we can move on to the next piece, which will be customizable accountability.
